Title: Nitrogen Assimilation Plays a Role in Balancing the Chloroplastic Glutathione Redox Potential Under High Light Conditions

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GS2 reduces de novo nitrate assimilation into glutamine/glutamate, increases PSII photoinhibition under high light, and prevents relaxation of chloroplastic glutathione redox potential during high light.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis (gln2); immunoblot; 15N-nitrate isotope labeling with LC-HRMS metabolite profiling; chlorophyll fluorescence (ΦPSII); chloroplast-targeted roGFP2 redox imaging; elevated CO2 test
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. gln2 mutants showed reduced GS2 protein abundance and lower 15N incorporation from nitrate into glutamine and glutamate than WT. 2. High light stimulated nitrate assimilation in WT, but this stimulation was reduced in gln2. 3. gln2 plants displayed stronger PSII photoinhibition under high light than WT. 4. In WT, chloroplastic roGFP2 oxidation relaxed during/after high light, whereas this relaxation was impaired in gln2, supporting a role for nitrogen assimilation in redox balancing.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Studied in leaves of 2–3-week-old plants under normal and high light
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: normal light; medium light (800 µmol m^-2 s^-1); high light (1200 µmol m^-2 s^-1); dark; elevated CO2 (700-900 ppm)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Maintains chloroplastic redox homeostasis and supports photoprotection under high light; glutamine/glutamate support nitrogen assimilation
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: gln2 plants were slightly smaller than WT (~70% leaf area) and showed increased vulnerability to high-light photoinhibition
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Improving chloroplastic GS2-dependent nitrogen assimilation may enhance photoprotection and redox stability under fluctuating/high light; useful target for optimizing nitrogen use and stress resilience
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-HRMS metabolite profiling of 15N-labeled glutamine and glutamate; no public accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper treats GS2 as part of the GS/GOGAT cycle with roles in both de novo nitrate assimilation and photorespiration, but elevated CO2 experiments support that the reported high-light redox phenotype mainly reflects de novo nitrogen assimilation rather than photorespiration.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GOGAT1 strongly reduces de novo nitrate assimilation, lowers photosynthetic efficiency, and abolishes high-light relaxation of chloroplastic glutathione redox potential.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis (glu1); immunoblot; 15N-nitrate isotope labeling with LC-HRMS metabolite profiling; chlorophyll fluorescence (ΦPSII); chloroplast-targeted roGFP2 redox imaging; elevated CO2 test
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. glu1 mutants showed reduced GOGAT1 protein abundance and markedly decreased 15N incorporation from nitrate into glutamine and glutamate. 2. Unlike WT, glu1 showed little to no stimulation of nitrate assimilation by high light. 3. glu1 exhibited chlorotic growth, lower basal/maximal PSII efficiency, and stronger high-light photoinhibition. 4. chl-roGFP2 oxidation relaxation seen in WT during declining light was absent during high-light periods in glu1, indicating that nitrogen assimilation contributes to chloroplastic redox recovery.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Studied in leaves of 2–3-week-old plants under normal and high light
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: normal light; medium light (800 µmol m^-2 s^-1); high light (1200 µmol m^-2 s^-1); dark; elevated CO2 (700-900 ppm)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Maintains chloroplastic redox homeostasis and supports photoprotection under high light; glutamate/glutamine support nitrogen assimilation
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: glu1 plants were small, chlorotic, and had reduced PSII efficiency even before high-light treatment
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining or enhancing chloroplastic GOGAT1 activity may improve electron sink capacity, redox balance, and light-stress tolerance, though strong perturbation can impair growth
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-HRMS metabolite profiling of 15N-labeled glutamine and glutamate; no public accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The discussion notes possible partial compensation by GOGAT2 or NADH-GOGAT, but these are background genes and not core experimental targets.
